Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and study for a few hours, but then I'd get distracted or something would come up with work or my extracurriculars. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I felt like I absolutely had to, not because I had some structured plan. I'd sometimes listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which felt productive but probably wasn't the most focused approach.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then when a deadline was breathing down my neck, I'd suddenly be cramming everything in. Being 27 and having other responsibilities means my schedule isn't as flexible as it was when I was younger. I couldn't commit to regular study blocks because life just kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in because I got an email reminder about an assignment I'd completely forgotten about. Occasionally I'd go in just to check if there was anything new, but that was rare. The gamified task stuff they added was kind of interesting and got me to log in a few extra times, I'll admit.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a week or two where I was logging in more regularly, especially when they introduced those game-like elements to some tasks. But it never stuck as a habit. I'd get busy with other things and suddenly realize I hadn't logged in for days. Routine and me don't really get along.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could put them on while commuting or doing chores, which made studying feel less like a burden. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in simpler terms than the official materials. Those peer interactions actually clarified some concepts I was struggling with.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and the longer video content. Sitting down to read dense PDFs just wasn't happening with my attention span and everything else going on. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The videos were too long and I'd zone out after the first ten minutes, so I stopped bothering.
Q7: If something felt like it would directly help me pass or if it was actually interesting, I'd engage with it. The gamified tasks were weirdly motivating because they felt less like homework and more like a challenge. But if something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to the assessments, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Optional activities felt exactly like that—optional. My brain basically categorized them as 'things I can ignore' immediately. Required stuff at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself to attempt it even if I didn't finish. With optional content, there was no external pressure, and I definitely need that external push to get things done.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the audio files and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near my computer. I also used some YouTube videos and podcasts that covered similar topics because sometimes outside sources explained things better. My notes were all in a physical notebook too, so none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely thinking about course concepts while walking around or at my part-time job, trying to piece things together in my head. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times but only the initial download would register. My best understanding often came from conversations with classmates in person, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the audio lectures once and then replay them for weeks without going back to the LMS. Same with some PDF summaries—I'd save them to my phone and reference them offline. The system probably thought I looked at something once and never again, but I was actually revisiting it repeatedly.
Q12: Around midterms everything just fell apart for me. My anxiety was through the roof, I was working extra shifts, and I felt completely overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. The more behind I got, the harder it was to even open the LMS because it just reminded me of how much I was failing. It became this vicious cycle.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all those incomplete tasks. Confusion about what I was supposed to be doing also made me avoid it—if I didn't understand something, I'd just close the tab rather than struggle through it. The competing responsibilities always won.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd open up materials and just stare at them while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but I wasn't actually processing anything—just going through the motions. It felt like I was studying, but nothing was actually sinking in because I was too anxious or distracted.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it worse—thinking about how much work I had to do made me want to avoid it even more. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's like my brain was protecting me from stress by creating more stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days and then frantically log in the night before something was due. It wasn't healthy or effective, but that panic was the only motivation that actually worked. Without a deadline looming, I just couldn't make myself prioritize coursework.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was kind of discouraging because it usually pointed out everything I was doing wrong. It made me less likely to engage, not more. I'd avoid checking my grades or comments because I already knew it wouldn't be good news. The gamified feedback was different though—those little achievement things felt less judgmental.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with eight online courses to juggle. I couldn't keep track of what was due when across all of them, so things slipped through the cracks constantly. If things had been more streamlined or if there were fewer moving parts, maybe I could have stayed on top of it better. The structure expected more organization than I was capable of.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier when I encountered them—they broke things into smaller, less intimidating chunks. But the sheer volume of content and the long video lectures made it harder. Having to navigate through multiple menus to find what I needed was frustrating too. Simple, clear pathways would have helped someone like me who was already struggling.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's anxious and keeps checking things, opening materials, trying to figure out what to do, but then getting overwhelmed and closing everything without actually completing anything. You can spend a lot of time in the system while accomplishing very little if your head's not in the right place. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some people are just more efficient or they're studying effectively outside the system. If someone downloads everything once and masters it offline, their LMS activity would look minimal but their understanding could be solid. I wish that was me, but my low activity matched my low performance pretty accurately.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether you actually understood anything or if you were just going through the motions. It misses all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with peers outside the platform. It definitely can't capture how stressed or anxious you are while using it.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and invested. I wanted to be engaged, I really did, but my anxiety and circumstances made it feel impossible most of the time. True engagement requires headspace I didn't have.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling internally while trying to keep up. It can't capture the guilt I felt every time I avoided logging in, or the anxiety attacks before deadlines. It also doesn't show that I was genuinely interested in some topics but just couldn't translate that interest into completed work. My engagement was there emotionally sometimes, even when my actions didn't reflect it.
